1: A shadow: Jethro, priest of Midian and Moses's father-in-law, heard all that God had done for Moses and for Israel. Jethro is a Gentile who acknowledges YHWH on the basis of what he hears about the exodus — he responds to testimony. This pattern anticipates the Gentile mission: 'faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ' (Romans 10:17). The Gentile who hears and comes prefigures the nations' response to the gospel proclamation.
2: A shadow: Jethro brings Zipporah (Moses's wife, sent back earlier) and Moses's two sons to the wilderness. The reunion of Moses with his family from Midian — a Gentile household joining the covenant people at Sinai — anticipates the ingathering of the scattered and the union of families across ethnic lines in the body of Christ (Ephesians 2:11-18, the dividing wall removed).
3: A theme: Moses's sons Gershom ('sojourner there') and Eliezer ('my God is help'). The names carry the theological freight of the entire exodus: Gershom names the sojourner-condition of exile from which Moses was saved; Eliezer names the divine rescue. Both dimensions — the human condition of alien-ness and the divine intervention that saves — are inscribed in the children Moses brings to Sinai.
4: A revelation of God: 'My God is help (Eliezer), for the God of my father was my help, and delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh.' The divine name embedded in the child's name is a perpetual confession of the exodus rescue — a walking theological statement that the God who delivered Moses is the helper of the vulnerable. Christ as the one who 'helps Abraham's offspring' (Hebrews 2:16) fulfills this helper-theology in its ultimate form.
5: A shadow: Jethro comes with Moses's wife and sons to the wilderness where Moses is camped, by the mountain of God. The Gentile's journey to the mountain of God anticipates Isaiah 2:2-3 — 'many peoples shall come and say: come, let us go up to the mountain of YHWH' — and the eschatological pilgrimage of the nations to Christ (Revelation 21:24-26, 'the nations will walk by its light').
6: A revelation of God: Jethro sends word ahead — 'I, your father-in-law Jethro, am coming to you with your wife and her two sons with her.' The advance announcement of arrival is a courtesy that implies mutual respect and anticipation. The relational texture of the Jethro narrative humanizes the story: the great lawgiver has family, has a Gentile father-in-law who loves him. Christ's own genealogy includes Gentile women (Matthew 1:5), and the incarnation was itself an advance-announced arrival (Isaiah 7:14).
7: A theme: Moses went out to meet his father-in-law, bowed down, and kissed him. Moses, YHWH's greatest servant, prostrates himself before a Gentile — a gesture of genuine honor that transcends ethnic hierarchy. The mutual honor between Jew and Gentile that the gospel creates (Ephesians 2:11-22) is prefigured in the remarkable image of Israel's lawgiver bowing before a Midianite priest.
8: A revelation of God: Moses told Jethro all that YHWH had done to Pharaoh and Egypt for Israel's sake, all the hardship they had faced, and how YHWH had delivered them. Moses's recounting of the exodus — the first sustained evangelistic proclamation in the Torah — is directed to a Gentile. The content: what YHWH did, the hardship endured, and the deliverance accomplished. This is the structure of all subsequent gospel proclamation: problem, cross, resurrection.
9: A shadow: Jethro rejoiced for all the good that YHWH had done to Israel, in that he had delivered them out of the hand of the Egyptians. The Gentile's rejoicing over Israel's deliverance anticipates Paul's appeal to the Gentiles in Romans 15:10 (quoting Deuteronomy 32:43): 'Rejoice, O Gentiles, with his people.' The nations' joy at YHWH's saving acts is the eschatological reality that Jethro's response prefigures.
10: A revelation of God: Jethro said, 'Blessed be YHWH, who has delivered you out of the hand of the Egyptians and out of the hand of Pharaoh and has delivered the people from under the hand of the Egyptians.' The Gentile's doxology — blessing YHWH — is the response that God seeks from all nations. The blessing of YHWH by a non-Israelite priest at the foot of Sinai is one of the most remarkable moments of Gentile worship in the entire Torah.
11: A revelation of God: 'Now I know that YHWH is greater than all gods, because in this affair they dealt arrogantly with the people.' Jethro's theology is shaped by what YHWH did: the exodus proves YHWH's supremacy over all other divine claims. This is exactly the conclusion the plagues were designed to produce (Exodus 7:5; Exodus 9:16). Jethro's confession — from outside the covenant — is the paradigm for the recognition that the nations will ultimately give to YHWH through Christ (Philippians 2:9-11).
12: A shadow: Jethro brought a burnt offering and sacrifices to God, and Aaron came with all the elders of Israel to eat bread before God with Moses's father-in-law. A Gentile priest offers sacrifice and presides at a meal with the elders of Israel before God. This covenant meal with a Gentile at the table anticipates the new covenant table of the Lord's Supper, where the dividing wall is abolished and Gentile and Jew eat together (Ephesians 2:14-16; 1 Corinthians 11:23-26).
13: A theme: the next day Moses sat to judge the people, and the people stood around Moses from morning till evening. The sole-mediator burden — Moses bearing all the judicial weight of a nation — reveals both the necessity and the impossibility of single-person mediation. Christ is the one mediator who does not grow weary (1 Timothy 2:5; Hebrews 7:25, 'always living to make intercession'), but the organizational lesson Jethro draws in vv14-26 becomes the structural basis of the church's distributed leadership.
14: A revelation of God: Jethro asks, 'What is this that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand around you from morning till evening?' The loving, practical concern of a father-in-law for his son-in-law's unsustainable burden reveals wisdom as a gift that God gives through ordinary human relationships. The distribution of wisdom through unexpected sources — here a Gentile — anticipates Paul's body metaphor: different members bear different gifts for the common good (1 Corinthians 12:7-11).
15: A theme: Moses answers, 'Because the people come to me to inquire of God; when they have a dispute, they come to me and I decide between one person and another, and I make them know the statutes of God and his laws.' Moses's self-description as the one who brings the people's cases to God and brings God's statutes to the people is the double function of the priest-mediator — intercession upward, instruction downward. Christ fulfills both: interceding for us (Hebrews 7:25) and teaching us the new covenant's ways (Matt 5-7; Hebrews 1:2).
16: A revelation of God: 'When they have a dispute, they come to me and I decide.' The judicial function of Moses — the one who knows God's statutes and applies them — anticipates Christ as the final judge (Acts 17:31; John 5:22,27). But unlike Moses who cannot sustain this role alone, Christ's judgment is perfect, unlimited, and does not require delegation.
17: A revelation of God: Jethro said, 'What you are doing is not good.' The plain-spoken correction of Moses by his Gentile father-in-law — and Moses's receptivity to it — reveals both the humility of true leadership and the democratic character of wisdom in Israel's community. God speaks through a Midianite priest to correct the greatest of Israel's leaders. The Spirit blows where it wills (John 3:8).
18: A theme: 'You and the people with you will certainly wear out, for the thing is too heavy for you. You are not able to do it alone.' The limit of human mediation — Moses's exhaustion, the people's exhaustion — is the precise point at which the insufficiency of the law-mediator becomes visible. Jethro's diagnosis of structural unsustainability points to the need for the mediator who 'always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25) without growing weary.
19: A theme: 'Listen now to my voice; I will give you advice, and God be with you! You shall represent the people before God and bring their cases to God.' The essential mediatorial role — representing the people before God — is what Moses is called to preserve even as he delegates judicial work. This core intercession is the function Christ preserves eternally. Every other judicial function can be distributed; the Son's intercession before the Father is unique.
20: A revelation of God: 'and you shall warn them about the statutes and the laws, and make them know the way in which they must walk and what they must do.' The teaching function of the mediator — making the way known — is fulfilled in Christ who is 'the way, and the truth, and the life' (John 14:6). Moses taught the way; Christ is the way.
21: A theme: choose able men who fear God, who are trustworthy and hate a bribe. The fourfold qualification for leadership — ability, fear of God, trustworthiness, incorruptibility — is the standard for church leadership in the NT (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9). Jethro's organizational wisdom, delivered by a Gentile, becomes the institutional template for the early church's appointment of deacons (Acts 6:3) and elders.
22: A theme: let them judge the people at all times, bringing only the hard cases to Moses. The principle of subsidiarity — decisions made at the lowest competent level — reflects a creation order in which different people bear different responsibilities. Paul applies the same principle to the church: the gifted members of the body each bear their own load (Galatians 6:5) while the harder cases are brought to the apostolic council (Acts 15).
23: A revelation of God: 'If you do this, God will direct you, you will be able to endure, and all this people also will go to their place in peace.' Jethro's organizational advice comes with a condition: if you do this — and God directs — then the outcome will be peace for the whole people. The connection between sound structure and shalom is a creation-order principle: God's design for human community when honored produces wellbeing. Disorder and overloading the mediator produces exhaustion and injustice.
24: A revelation of God: Moses listened to the voice of his father-in-law and did all that he had said. Moses's receptivity to counsel from a Gentile is a model of the humility that characterizes the true servant of God. Christ himself, though the Son, 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8) — the pattern of learning from others, even in the humility of the incarnation, is rooted in this narrative.
25: A theme: Moses chose able men out of all Israel and made them heads over the people — rulers of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens. The four-tier hierarchy (thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens) creates a scalable structure for a nation of 600,000. The church's organizational polity — elders, deacons, small groups — mirrors this scalable structure. The principle that God's people need ordered community, not only immediate access to the great mediator, runs through both Testaments.
26: A theme: the able men judged the people at all times; the hard cases they brought to Moses, but any small matter they decided themselves. The delegation of judicial authority while reserving ultimate appeal for the greatest mediator is the structure of the church's relationship to Christ: ordinary matters are handled by the community in Christ's name (Matthew 18:15-20); ultimate appeal belongs to him as the final judge (Acts 17:31).
27: A shadow: Moses let his father-in-law depart, and he went away to his own country. Jethro's departure after his contribution — the meal, the worship, the organizational wisdom — is the departure of the Gentile consultant who has served his purpose and returns to his people. The pattern anticipates the wider mission: Gentiles encounter the God of Israel, acknowledge his supremacy, contribute wisdom, and then carry the testimony back to their own people. 'This is how all the families of the earth shall be blessed' (Genesis 12:3) — through encounter with the God of Jacob's servant.
